The techniques you'll learn in this course will be on a more assertive front. There's an intentionally designed sequence here. Tactical empathy must precede assertion. It's to precursor for effective assertion. Without it, you're threatening, offensive, and perceived as a hostage taker yourself. The success rates for this type of negotiator go hand in hand with a high degree of implementation failure. With empathy, you're considered a straight shooter, someone who is honest and clear, but in an emotionally intelligent way. Your deal success rates are higher and your deal implementation rates are even higher still. The reality is, you can't make great deals without letting the other side know what you need. It's that simple. You can't be a great partner if you are unable to look out for yourself in an interaction. The strategies we'll introduce you to in this course have the potential to be so influential, we refer to them as bending reality. Some people may shy away from them because they feel they're manipulative. Is it misleading if you can interact with someone in a way that makes them feel like agreeing with you? Don't get me wrong, we don't want you to use your powers for evil. These tools are used by the best negotiators because they simply recognize human psyche as it is. Ignore human nature at your peril. The most powerful bending reality concept revolves around loss and our reaction to it. We'll dig into that in this course. Power, influence and manipulation are words that are good or bad primarily based on what they're used for. Use them for destructive purposes and you're sowing the seeds for retaliation. Use them for positive purposes and you're sowing the seeds for your long term success. There's a famous saying from an early Goldman Sachs executive, Gus Levy. Greedy yes, but long term greedy. If perception is reality and their perception doesn't match yours, where are you left by trying to explain your perception to them? These tactical empathy skills are designed to number one, find out what the other side's perception is so you can better describe to them what they see. And number two, give you the ability to alter that view or bend their reality. Those of you who think that logic and argument is what bends the other side's reality, I would imagine they're finding that process frustrating. Or better yet, you have a tendency to describe many of your counterparts as unreasonable. Another process also guaranteed to frustrate you is compromise. Masquerading is win-win or splitting the difference. We often do this because it seems fair, the F word. There's two major problems with these ideas. First, compromise is a formula for failure. It's the idea of let's take some of your idea, some of my idea, and force them together. For example, you and your spouse don't know whether to paint your bedroom blue or yellow, so you compromise and paint it green. Second, compromise always involves giving something up, also known as loss. Our view of what we give up will always exceed value placed on it by the other side. Imagine the downward spiral that puts you into. Some of you have already experienced this at some point. This course is designed to not only teach you how to see into the subterranean world of unspoken needs and thoughts of your counterparts, but hopefully it will allow you to reflect on yourself too. Because once you dip below the surface, you'll discover an entire universe of variables that can be leveraged to change your counterparts' needs and expectations. From using someone's fear of deadlines to our misunderstood relationship with fairness, there are always ways to bend our counterparts' reality so it conforms to what we ultimately want to negotiate. A final note. Everyone has irrational blind spots, hidden needs, and undeveloped notions. Even us at Black Swan. We all have patterns of behavior. Look for these patterns and then use the tools we've given you to make great deals.
